texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regarding the rapid progress in preparing new materials, with superior properties for membranes manufacturing, membrane separation in recent decades, has attracted more interest. Since membrane systems have many advantages like small size, reliable and effective performance and simplicity of operation and maintenance, they have been employed as an alternative method for conventional separation methods. Also in hybrid systems advantages of both membrane and conventional separation methods can be achieved [1,2].
texts.text: Regarding the rapid progress in preparing new materials, with superior properties for membranes manufacturing, membrane separation in recent decades, has attracted more interest. Since membrane systems have many advantages like small size, reliable and effective performance and simplicity of operation and maintenance, they have been employed as an alternative method for conventional separation methods. Also in hybrid systems advantages of both membrane and conventional separation methods can be achieved [1,2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrogen separation from its mixtures with other gases like methane, carbon monoxide and nitrogen with membranes in syngas ratio adjustment or in hydrogen recovery from hydrocarbons in refineries, in different industries is developing rapidly. Syngas is a useful and important gas in petrochemical industries. In order to use it in different applications, the H2/CO ratio in this gas should be reduced to specific levels. In some processes like methanol, acetic acid and oxo alcohol production, this mixture is needed with a proper ratio, which is often less than its original ratio [3]. Process equipment and methods of separation, in membrane processes are inherently much simpler than those in traditional separation methods. This is especially true for separation of gaseous mixtures such as carbon monoxide
texts.text: Hydrogen separation from its mixtures with other gases like methane, carbon monoxide and nitrogen with membranes in syngas ratio adjustment or in hydrogen recovery from hydrocarbons in refineries, in different industries is developing rapidly. Syngas is a useful and important gas in petrochemical industries. In order to use it in different applications, the H2/CO ratio in this gas should be reduced to specific levels. In some processes like methanol, acetic acid and oxo alcohol production, this mixture is needed with a proper ratio, which is often less than its original ratio [3]. Process equipment and methods of separation, in membrane processes are inherently much simpler than those in traditional separation methods. This is especially true for separation of gaseous mixtures such as carbon monoxide

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, separation of hydrogen from carbon monoxide by a polyimide hollow fiber membrane module was investigated. Polyimide showed high selectivity for hydrogen relative to carbon monoxide at different operational conditions. Effects of temperature, pressure and stage-cut on permeability of gases and membrane selectivity were experimentally studied. The experiments were carried out employing mixtures containing: 0, 10, 25, 40 and 50% carbon monoxide. Feed temperatures and pressures were varied between 20 and 80 ◦ C and 5-9bar, respectively. Pure gas permeances through the membrane were found to be nearly constant at different pressures. Also, the results confirmed the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the membrane and showed reduction of hydrogen permeance in mixed gas experiments due to competitive sorption and concentration polarization effects. Also, membrane area for a specific separation was calculated using a new developed model. The optimum condition which results in minimum required area for the membrane can be determined in this way.
texts.text: In this work, separation of hydrogen from carbon monoxide by a polyimide hollow fiber membrane module was investigated. Polyimide showed high selectivity for hydrogen relative to carbon monoxide at different operational conditions. Effects of temperature, pressure and stage-cut on permeability of gases and membrane selectivity were experimentally studied. The experiments were carried out employing mixtures containing: 0, 10, 25, 40 and 50% carbon monoxide. Feed temperatures and pressures were varied between 20 and 80 ◦ C and 5-9bar, respectively. Pure gas permeances through the membrane were found to be nearly constant at different pressures. Also, the results confirmed the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the membrane and showed reduction of hydrogen permeance in mixed gas experiments due to competitive sorption and concentration polarization effects. Also, membrane area for a specific separation was calculated using a new developed model. The optimum condition which results in minimum required area for the membrane can be determined in this way.

texts.orig: and hydrogen which requires cryogenic temperatures when separated by condensation, distillation or adsorption [4]. The first commercial application for the PRISM polysulfone hollow fiber membrane developed by Monsanto, was syngas ratio adjustment [5].
texts.text: and hydrogen which requires cryogenic temperatures when separated by condensation, distillation or adsorption [4]. The first commercial application for the PRISM polysulfone hollow fiber membrane developed by Monsanto, was syngas ratio adjustment [5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyimide is a glassy polymer which is known as a suitable material for gas separation. This polymer is widely used in industrial applications of gas separation. However, only limited data are available on gas separation properties of such membranes for H2/CO separation. For example, most of the published data on commercially available polyimide hollow fibers are about CO2/N2 separation [6-8]. Since polyimide has a high glass transition temperature, it can be used for performing experiments at higher temperatures than other polymeric membranes.
texts.text: Polyimide is a glassy polymer which is known as a suitable material for gas separation. This polymer is widely used in industrial applications of gas separation. However, only limited data are available on gas separation properties of such membranes for H2/CO separation. For example, most of the published data on commercially available polyimide hollow fibers are about CO2/N2 separation [6-8]. Since polyimide has a high glass transition temperature, it can be used for performing experiments at higher temperatures than other polymeric membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In syngas ratio adjustment with membranes, retentate is the desired product which is present at high pressure (approximately feed pressure). Hydrogen permeates selectively through the membrane and therefore the H2/CO ratio in the retentate stream decreases. This process also can be carried out with cryogenic or pressure swing adsorption processes [9]. PSA and cryogenic separations are not very simple methods for gas separation. In addition they need expensive equipments and are very energy consuming processes. However, membrane separation is a simple process and uses much lower energy. Also, the main product of the H2/CO separation, which is H2 /CO mixture with a modified and desired ratio, is available at high pressure in this separation method.
texts.text: In syngas ratio adjustment with membranes, retentate is the desired product which is present at high pressure (approximately feed pressure). Hydrogen permeates selectively through the membrane and therefore the H2/CO ratio in the retentate stream decreases. This process also can be carried out with cryogenic or pressure swing adsorption processes [9]. PSA and cryogenic separations are not very simple methods for gas separation. In addition they need expensive equipments and are very energy consuming processes. However, membrane separation is a simple process and uses much lower energy. Also, the main product of the H2/CO separation, which is H2 /CO mixture with a modified and desired ratio, is available at high pressure in this separation method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work the properties of polyimide for separation of hydrogen from syngas was experimentally investigated at different feed conditions.Incaseoftemperatureeffect,theresultswereconsistent with the literature. Temperature increase enhances the hydrogen permeance in polyimide composite membranes [14]. The results showed that carbon monoxide plasticizes the polymer and thus its permeance increases with increasing pressure. However, in mixed gas experiments, competitive sorption and concentration polarization effects reduce hydrogen permeance at elevated pressures. Also, required membrane area for a specific separation was calculated by simulation at different conditions, using the pure gas permeancesobtainedfromexperimentaldata. The component permeances were obtained as functions of temperature and pressure and optimum operational conditions were determined from the experimental data.
texts.text: In this work the properties of polyimide for separation of hydrogen from syngas was experimentally investigated at different feed conditions.Incaseoftemperatureeffect,theresultswereconsistent with the literature. Temperature increase enhances the hydrogen permeance in polyimide composite membranes [14]. The results showed that carbon monoxide plasticizes the polymer and thus its permeance increases with increasing pressure. However, in mixed gas experiments, competitive sorption and concentration polarization effects reduce hydrogen permeance at elevated pressures. Also, required membrane area for a specific separation was calculated by simulation at different conditions, using the pure gas permeancesobtainedfromexperimentaldata. The component permeances were obtained as functions of temperature and pressure and optimum operational conditions were determined from the experimental data.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A polyimide membrane with a molecular structure, as shown in Fig. 1, was used in all the experiments. This membrane is a thin composite membrane whose skin and support layers are both composed of the same polyimide by going through the polyamic acid-polyimide cycle twice; the first time to prepare a solventresistant porous support membrane with an ultrathin film of polyamic acid which is then condensed to yield the fully imidized composite membrane [5]. This polyimide module was made by UBE Industries Ltd. for laboratory experiments. For carrying out the experiments, a gas permeation set up, was assembled. Using this set up, temperature, pressure and flow rate could be adjusted and controlled. A schematic of the gas permeation set up is shown in Fig. 2. In this system, hydrogen and carbon monoxide cylinders with purity of 99.99 and 99.50%, respectively, were used as feed gases.
texts.text: A polyimide membrane with a molecular structure, as shown in Fig. 1, was used in all the experiments. This membrane is a thin composite membrane whose skin and support layers are both composed of the same polyimide by going through the polyamic acid-polyimide cycle twice; the first time to prepare a solventresistant porous support membrane with an ultrathin film of polyamic acid which is then condensed to yield the fully imidized composite membrane [5]. This polyimide module was made by UBE Industries Ltd. for laboratory experiments. For carrying out the experiments, a gas permeation set up, was assembled. Using this set up, temperature, pressure and flow rate could be adjusted and controlled. A schematic of the gas permeation set up is shown in Fig. 2. In this system, hydrogen and carbon monoxide cylinders with purity of 99.99 and 99.50%, respectively, were used as feed gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane module used in the experiments, was a hollow fiber module. The effective surface area of the membrane was 12.56 cm 2 . The length of the fiber was 1 m. There was only one fiber in the module. Feed was introduced at the shell side of the module and permeate was collected from the core side and feed/permeate flow pattern was countercurrent.
texts.text: Membrane module used in the experiments, was a hollow fiber module. The effective surface area of the membrane was 12.56 cm 2 . The length of the fiber was 1 m. There was only one fiber in the module. Feed was introduced at the shell side of the module and permeate was collected from the core side and feed/permeate flow pattern was countercurrent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas experiments were carried out using hydrogen and carbon monoxide, respectively. These experiments were carried out at ambient temperature and different feed pressures (3, 5, 7 and 9bar). Permeate was always at atmospheric pressure. Mixed gas experiments were carried out using mixtures containing 0, 10, 25, 40 and 50% carbon monoxide, respectively. Feed pressure was varied from 5 to 9 bar and feed temperature was varied from 20 to 80 ◦ C. Some of the experiments were repeated and the accuracy of results was investigated. The average amounts of two or three similar measurements were plotted in figures. Maximum error was calculated to be 8% in the experimental results.
texts.text: Pure gas experiments were carried out using hydrogen and carbon monoxide, respectively. These experiments were carried out at ambient temperature and different feed pressures (3, 5, 7 and 9bar). Permeate was always at atmospheric pressure. Mixed gas experiments were carried out using mixtures containing 0, 10, 25, 40 and 50% carbon monoxide, respectively. Feed pressure was varied from 5 to 9 bar and feed temperature was varied from 20 to 80 ◦ C. Some of the experiments were repeated and the accuracy of results was investigated. The average amounts of two or three similar measurements were plotted in figures. Maximum error was calculated to be 8% in the experimental results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All measurements were done after 20-30min, when the system reached to its steady state at that specific condition. As shown in Fig. 2, hydrogen and carbon monoxide gases were mixed with appropriate ratio in a mixing vessel and then the mixture was sent to the module.
texts.text: All measurements were done after 20-30min, when the system reached to its steady state at that specific condition. As shown in Fig. 2, hydrogen and carbon monoxide gases were mixed with appropriate ratio in a mixing vessel and then the mixture was sent to the module.

texts.text: Also, some experiments were carried out to investigate the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the polyimide membrane with pure carbon monoxide, using a fresh polyimide membrane module. These experiments were extended for 8h and carbon monoxide permeance was measured every 1h.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are some determining factors which affect the results of pure and mixed gas experiments and observed trends for test results can be explained by their combined effects. These parameters are: plasticization effect, effect of non-ideality of gases, effect of competitive sorption in mixtures and concentration polarization effect. Plasticization effect tends to increase both gas permeances. However, effects of non-ideality and competitive sorption tend to decrease the gas permeances [10,11,15]. Accumulation of slow permeating molecules like CO and depletion of fast permeating molecules like H2 on the membrane surface can enhance the concentration polarization effect [11]. The former decreases the hydrogen permeance due to the competitive sorption effect. Pure andmixedgasexperimentalresults can now be discussed using the effects of these parameters.
texts.text: There are some determining factors which affect the results of pure and mixed gas experiments and observed trends for test results can be explained by their combined effects. These parameters are: plasticization effect, effect of non-ideality of gases, effect of competitive sorption in mixtures and concentration polarization effect. Plasticization effect tends to increase both gas permeances. However, effects of non-ideality and competitive sorption tend to decrease the gas permeances [10,11,15]. Accumulation of slow permeating molecules like CO and depletion of fast permeating molecules like H2 on the membrane surface can enhance the concentration polarization effect [11]. The former decreases the hydrogen permeance due to the competitive sorption effect. Pure andmixedgasexperimentalresults can now be discussed using the effects of these parameters.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.1. Effect of pressure
texts.text: 4.2.1. Effect of pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Fig. 4, effects of feed pressure on hydrogen and carbon monoxide permeances in mixtures with 75% hydrogen and 25% carbon monoxide are presented. These experiments were carried out at different feed flow rates.
texts.text: In Fig. 4, effects of feed pressure on hydrogen and carbon monoxide permeances in mixtures with 75% hydrogen and 25% carbon monoxide are presented. These experiments were carried out at different feed flow rates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Comparingtheresultsofpureandmixedgasexperimentsshows that the carbon monoxide permeance evaluated at mixed experiments is higher than its permeance at pure experiments. This can be related to the carbon monoxide plasticization effect. The mixed gas experiments were carried out after pure gas experiments. Since the membrane was in contact with carbon monoxide in pure gas experiments,plasticizationeffectenhanceditspermeanceinmixed gas experiments. For hydrogen, in contrast, at pressures equal to 7bar and higher, pure gas permeance was higher than mixed gas permeance at the same partial pressure.
texts.text: Comparingtheresultsofpureandmixedgasexperimentsshows that the carbon monoxide permeance evaluated at mixed experiments is higher than its permeance at pure experiments. This can be related to the carbon monoxide plasticization effect. The mixed gas experiments were carried out after pure gas experiments. Since the membrane was in contact with carbon monoxide in pure gas experiments,plasticizationeffectenhanceditspermeanceinmixed gas experiments. For hydrogen, in contrast, at pressures equal to 7bar and higher, pure gas permeance was higher than mixed gas permeance at the same partial pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be observed, in the case of the mixed gas permeation experiments, increasing feed pressure increases carbon monoxide permeance. This behavior is similar to the trend observed in the puregasexperiments.Thisincreaseincarbonmonoxidepermeance can be due to the accelerated plasticization of the membrane with carbon monoxide. This effect tends to increase both gas permeances.However,ascanbeobserved,hydrogenpermeancedecreases with increasing feed pressure. This behavior was similar for all mixed feed flow rates. As mentioned before, in addition to the plasticization effect, there are competitive sorption and concentration polarization effects in the mixed gas experiments. The effect of competitive sorption tends to decrease hydrogen permeances [11].
texts.text: As can be observed, in the case of the mixed gas permeation experiments, increasing feed pressure increases carbon monoxide permeance. This behavior is similar to the trend observed in the puregasexperiments.Thisincreaseincarbonmonoxidepermeance can be due to the accelerated plasticization of the membrane with carbon monoxide. This effect tends to increase both gas permeances.However,ascanbeobserved,hydrogenpermeancedecreases with increasing feed pressure. This behavior was similar for all mixed feed flow rates. As mentioned before, in addition to the plasticization effect, there are competitive sorption and concentration polarization effects in the mixed gas experiments. The effect of competitive sorption tends to decrease hydrogen permeances [11].

texts.orig: 4.1. Pure gas permeation tests
texts.text: 4.1. Pure gas permeation tests

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas experiments were carried out using hydrogen, carbon monoxideandnitrogen.Theseexperimentswerecarriedoutatfeed pressures equal to 5, 7 and 9 bar, respectively. The permeances of these pure gases and also permselectivity for H2/CO are presented in Fig. 3.
texts.text: Pure gas experiments were carried out using hydrogen, carbon monoxideandnitrogen.Theseexperimentswerecarriedoutatfeed pressures equal to 5, 7 and 9 bar, respectively. The permeances of these pure gases and also permselectivity for H2/CO are presented in Fig. 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be observed in this figure, pure gas permeances for hydrogen remaines relatively constant with increasing pressure. On the other hand, the hydrogen permeance is pressure independentinthisdensepolyimidemembrane.Ingeneral,gaspermeances through dense polymeric membranes should be independent of pressure, because they are normalized by pressure. However, the data taken for carbon monoxide experiments show an increase in its permeance with increasing pressure. This increase can be due to the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the exposed membrane. Because, hydrogen permeance remains constant and carbon monoxide permeance increases only slightly, the ideal selectivity slighty decreases.
texts.text: As can be observed in this figure, pure gas permeances for hydrogen remaines relatively constant with increasing pressure. On the other hand, the hydrogen permeance is pressure independentinthisdensepolyimidemembrane.Ingeneral,gaspermeances through dense polymeric membranes should be independent of pressure, because they are normalized by pressure. However, the data taken for carbon monoxide experiments show an increase in its permeance with increasing pressure. This increase can be due to the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the exposed membrane. Because, hydrogen permeance remains constant and carbon monoxide permeance increases only slightly, the ideal selectivity slighty decreases.

texts.orig: Fig. 6. Effect of feed pressure on hydrogen permeance (feed: 60% H2 and 40% CO; temperature=25 ◦ C).
texts.text: Fig. 6. Effect of feed pressure on hydrogen permeance (feed: 60% H2 and 40% CO; temperature=25 ◦ C).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2.2. Effect of stage cut
texts.text: 4.2.2. Effect of stage cut

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To study the effect of stage cut on the membrane performance, someexperimentsatconstantfeedpressureanddifferentfeedflow rates, were carried out. As mentioned before, stage cut is the ratio of permeate flow to feed flow and as a result, increasing feed flow rate decreases stage cut. The effect of stage cut on membrane selectivity at different feed pressures is presented in Fig. 7.
texts.text: To study the effect of stage cut on the membrane performance, someexperimentsatconstantfeedpressureanddifferentfeedflow rates, were carried out. As mentioned before, stage cut is the ratio of permeate flow to feed flow and as a result, increasing feed flow rate decreases stage cut. The effect of stage cut on membrane selectivity at different feed pressures is presented in Fig. 7.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As mentioned, as pressure decreases, the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the membrane diminishes. Also, concentration polarization effect is less significant at lower pressures. Therefore, at lower pressures, hydrogen permeance decreases slower. As a result, membrane permselectivity increases with decreasing pressure. Also, it can be observed that moving along with the module, or in other words, by increasing stage cut, membrane selectivity decreases. It can be due to the fact that, by moving along the module, hydrogen concentration in the retentate side, and as a result, its permeance decreases. In fact, increasing feed flow rate (decreasing stage cut), delays attaining to equilibrium and therefore increases the membrane selectivity. This reduction in the membrane selectivity is more significant at higher feed pressures. In Fig. 8, the effect of stage cut on hydrogen and carbon monoxide compositions in the retentate, is depicted.
texts.text: As mentioned, as pressure decreases, the plasticization effect of carbon monoxide on the membrane diminishes. Also, concentration polarization effect is less significant at lower pressures. Therefore, at lower pressures, hydrogen permeance decreases slower. As a result, membrane permselectivity increases with decreasing pressure. Also, it can be observed that moving along with the module, or in other words, by increasing stage cut, membrane selectivity decreases. It can be due to the fact that, by moving along the module, hydrogen concentration in the retentate side, and as a result, its permeance decreases. In fact, increasing feed flow rate (decreasing stage cut), delays attaining to equilibrium and therefore increases the membrane selectivity. This reduction in the membrane selectivity is more significant at higher feed pressures. In Fig. 8, the effect of stage cut on hydrogen and carbon monoxide compositions in the retentate, is depicted.

texts.text: 4.2.3. Effect of temperature

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Effect of temperature on the membrane performance was also investigated. The results are presented in Fig. 9.
texts.text: Effect of temperature on the membrane performance was also investigated. The results are presented in Fig. 9.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Plasticization of membrane
texts.text: 4.3. Plasticization of membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To better understand the plasticization effect, for a period of 8 h, carbon monoxide permeance was measured every 1h. This experiment was performed using a fresh polyimide membrane module with pure CO feed. The permeance-time curves at two different pressures for carbon monoxide are shown in Fig. 10.
texts.text: To better understand the plasticization effect, for a period of 8 h, carbon monoxide permeance was measured every 1h. This experiment was performed using a fresh polyimide membrane module with pure CO feed. The permeance-time curves at two different pressures for carbon monoxide are shown in Fig. 10.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results show that carbon monoxide permeance increases with time. It shows that carbon monoxide permeance increases at first 4 h, and then it remains constant, at both feed pressures. Increasing carbon monoxide permeance at first 4 h, indicates accelerated plasticization of membrane by carbon monoxide. However, the membrane plasticization completes after 4 h, and as a result, carbon monoxide permeance remains constant. In fact, the membrane plasticization reaches to equilibrium at this time.
texts.text: The results show that carbon monoxide permeance increases with time. It shows that carbon monoxide permeance increases at first 4 h, and then it remains constant, at both feed pressures. Increasing carbon monoxide permeance at first 4 h, indicates accelerated plasticization of membrane by carbon monoxide. However, the membrane plasticization completes after 4 h, and as a result, carbon monoxide permeance remains constant. In fact, the membrane plasticization reaches to equilibrium at this time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Also, some mixed gas experiments were repeated after doing all designed experiments, using the original membrane module. Results of these repeated experiments are shown in Table 1.
texts.text: Also, some mixed gas experiments were repeated after doing all designed experiments, using the original membrane module. Results of these repeated experiments are shown in Table 1.

texts.orig: Ascanbeobserved,carbonmonoxidepermeanceintestnumber 2 is larger than that in test number 1. The results certainly show plasticization of the membrane after being in contact with carbon monoxide in all designed experiments.

texts.orig: Required membraneareaforaspecific separation (feed: 75% H2; retentate: 50% H2), was calculated at some operational conditions. This was carried out using an earlier developed model [16]. In this model, the membrane was divided into N equal stages and differential mass balance equations were written for each component in each step. These equations were solved using a tridiagonal matrix. The effect of being cocurrent or countercurrent flow pattern in the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: module was also considered in calculations. For solving the differential mass balance equations, cross flow pattern was supposed as an initial guess, then the governing equations for cocurrent or countercurrent flow patterns were solved with this initial guess and solving procedure was repeated until convergence. For convergence, the total flow rate of retentate and permeate should remain constant in subsequent solving steps. Details of the model were explained elsewhere [16]. Required permeances for simulation were obtained from the experimental data. In industrial processes, both selectivity and permeance should have acceptable values to minimize the required membrane area, and as a result, the cost of membrane unit. In Table 2, the results of required membrane area calculated according to some experimental data, are presented.
texts.text: module was also considered in calculations. For solving the differential mass balance equations, cross flow pattern was supposed as an initial guess, then the governing equations for cocurrent or countercurrent flow patterns were solved with this initial guess and solving procedure was repeated until convergence. For convergence, the total flow rate of retentate and permeate should remain constant in subsequent solving steps. Details of the model were explained elsewhere [16]. Required permeances for simulation were obtained from the experimental data. In industrial processes, both selectivity and permeance should have acceptable values to minimize the required membrane area, and as a result, the cost of membrane unit. In Table 2, the results of required membrane area calculated according to some experimental data, are presented.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be observed, in this case, increasing temperature at higher pressures favors the separation, because it reduces the required membrane area. In each case, regarding to the flow rate, purity of the product (in this work, the retentate stream) and the required membrane area, the optimum operational condition can be obtained. The optimum operational condition was investigated from the membrane required area point of view. In fact, it must be mentioned that there is a trade off between temperature, pressure, membrane area and separation efficiency.
texts.text: As can be observed, in this case, increasing temperature at higher pressures favors the separation, because it reduces the required membrane area. In each case, regarding to the flow rate, purity of the product (in this work, the retentate stream) and the required membrane area, the optimum operational condition can be obtained. The optimum operational condition was investigated from the membrane required area point of view. In fact, it must be mentioned that there is a trade off between temperature, pressure, membrane area and separation efficiency.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The polyimide membrane showed high potential for hydrogen separation from carbon monoxide (syngas ratio adjustment). Experimental results showed selectivity between 15 and 30 for hydrogenrelativetocarbonmonoxide.Also,theresultsshowedthat carbon monoxide plasticizes the polyimide membrane at high-feed pressures. Also, concentration polarization and competitive sorption effect was claimed to be responsible for reduction of hydrogen permeance with increasing feed pressure. Effect of temperature on the membrane plasticization was found to be less significant than that on diffusion coefficient of hydrogen molecules. Therefore, increasing temperature increases hydrogen permeance, and as a result, the membrane selectivity.
texts.text: The polyimide membrane showed high potential for hydrogen separation from carbon monoxide (syngas ratio adjustment). Experimental results showed selectivity between 15 and 30 for hydrogenrelativetocarbonmonoxide.Also,theresultsshowedthat carbon monoxide plasticizes the polyimide membrane at high-feed pressures. Also, concentration polarization and competitive sorption effect was claimed to be responsible for reduction of hydrogen permeance with increasing feed pressure. Effect of temperature on the membrane plasticization was found to be less significant than that on diffusion coefficient of hydrogen molecules. Therefore, increasing temperature increases hydrogen permeance, and as a result, the membrane selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using the permeances obtained from experimental data for the pure gas experiments, the required membrane area for a specific separation was calculated with simulation using a mathematical model.Withthesimulation,ateachoperationalcondition,themin- imum membrane area and/or the optimum operational condition could be estimated.
texts.text: Using the permeances obtained from experimental data for the pure gas experiments, the required membrane area for a specific separation was calculated with simulation using a mathematical model.Withthesimulation,ateachoperationalcondition,themin- imum membrane area and/or the optimum operational condition could be estimated.
